story.title: Dr. Chen Models Your Moon Weight
story.text:
Hello, I am Dr. Sarah Chen. I am a research scientist. I build computer models to understand complex systems. Today, we will model gravity to answer a big question. Why do you weigh less on the Moon?

First, know that your mass never changes. Your mass is all the matter that makes up your body. But your weight can change. Weight is the force from gravitational pull acting on your mass.

A planet’s mass affects its gravity. Earth has a large planet mass. This creates a strong gravitational pull. This strong pull gives you a higher object weight. This makes your jump height on Earth feel normal.

The Moon is much smaller than Earth. It has much less planet mass. This means its gravitational pull is weaker. On the Moon, the pull on your mass is small. So, your object weight is much less. With weaker gravity, your jump height is much greater. You could jump six times higher! My models help astronauts prepare for these different forces.
think_abouts: Why does your mass stay the same everywhere, but your weight can change?
How does a planet's mass affect how high you can jump on it?
story.go_deeper: Jupiter is much more massive than Earth. What could you predict about your weight and jump height on Jupiter?

margin_defs.definition: simple versions of real things to help us study them
the 'stuff' that everything is made of
pushes or pulls that can change an object's motion

check_prompts: The main reason you weigh less on the Moon is because the Moon has less ___.

vocabulary.definition: The force that pulls objects with mass toward each other.
vocabulary.use_sentence: The Earth's strong gravitational pull keeps the Moon in orbit.

vocabulary.definition: The total amount of matter, or 'stuff,' in an object.
vocabulary.use_sentence: Your mass is the same whether you are on Earth or on the Moon.

vocabulary.definition: The measure of the force of gravity pulling on an object's mass.
vocabulary.use_sentence: Your weight is less on the Moon because its gravity is weaker.

vocabulary.definition: A scientific rule that says gravity gets much weaker as you get farther away from an object.
vocabulary.use_sentence: The inverse square law explains why planets far from the sun feel its gravity less.

discussion_cards.prompt: Dr. Chen uses models to understand systems like gravity. What is another complex system you could use a model to understand? (Examples: weather, traffic, an ecosystem)
discussion_cards.frame: A model could help me understand the system of ___ by showing how ___.

discussion_cards.prompt: If you were designing a new sport to be played on the Moon, what would it be like? How would the Moon's weaker gravity and your lower weight change the game?
discussion_cards.frame: A sport on the Moon would be different because ___. I would design a game where players could ___.

reflection_frames.knew: Before reading, I already knew that ___
reflection_frames.learned: From the story, I learned that ___
